name: Valladolid, Simancas, and Tordesillas Loop
name_original: Tordesillas desde Valladolid – circular por Castillo de Simancas
description: This moderate 44.3-mile road cycling route from Valladolid explores historical Simancas and Tordesillas, offering scenic views along the Due
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Arroyo De La Encomienda traverse a landscape characterized by the Duero River basin, featuring generally flat to gently rolling terrain. The region offers a mix of open agricultural fields, rural roads connecting historical towns, and occasional river valleys. Elevation changes are typically gradual, making it suitable for sustained road cycling efforts. The area's geography provides opportunities for long-distance rides with consistent gradients. Best road cycling routes around Arroyo De La Encomienda The most popular road cycling route is Valladolid, Simancas, and Tordesillas Loop, a 44.3 miles (71.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 11 minutes to complete, connecting several historical towns. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Church of Santa María de Wamba – Geria Road loop from Arroyo de la Encomienda, a moderate 42.2 miles (68.0 km) path. This route passes through historical sites and agricultural areas. Local road cyclists also love the Medieval Bridge of Tordesillas – Geria Road loop from Arroyo de la Encomienda, a 46.3 miles (74.5 km) trail leading through historical bridges and river valleys, often completed in about 3 hours 19 minutes. Road cycling around Arroyo De La Encomienda is defined by rural roads, historical towns, and agricultural landscapes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Arroyo De La Encomienda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. More than 5,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Arroyo De La Encomienda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Arroyo De La Encomienda?
A: There are over 110 road cycling routes in the Arroyo De La Encomienda area, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in this region?
A: The region around Arroyo De La Encomienda is characterized by the Duero River basin, featuring generally flat to gently rolling terrain. You'll encounter open agricultural fields, rural roads connecting historical towns, and occasional river valleys, with mostly gradual elevation changes.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Arroyo De La Encomienda offers around 20 easy road cycling routes. These routes typically feature minimal elevation gain and well-maintained rural roads, making them ideal for beginners or those seeking a relaxed ride. While specific family-friendly routes aren't highlighted, the generally flat terrain is suitable for all ages.

Q: Can I find challenging routes for advanced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For advanced cyclists looking for more challenging rides, there are 8 difficult routes available. These routes often include longer distances and more significant elevation changes, providing a good test of endurance and skill.

Q: Are there any loop routes for road cycling around Arroyo De La Encomienda?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40638958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María de Wamba – View of Castroverde de Cerrato loop from Arroyo de la Encomienda&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate 41.6-mile (67 km) ride through scenic landscapes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40638241" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María de Wamba – Torrelobatón Castle loop from Arroyo de la Encomienda&lt;/a&gt;, covering 38.7 miles (62 km).

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes often pass through or near historical towns and sites. You can explore attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3044187" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuensaldaña Castle&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5837382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Simancas&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4394423" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of Valladolid&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also feature historical bridges and religious buildings like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5707746" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María la Antigua&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Arroyo De La Encomienda?
A: Given the region's climate, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures and less extreme weather. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring colder temperatures, though routes remain accessible.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or cafes along the routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in Arroyo De La Encomienda connect various towns and villages. These settlements typically offer opportunities to stop for refreshments, including cafes, restaurants, and local shops, especially in larger towns like Valladolid or Tordesillas.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Arroyo De La Encomienda?
A: The road cycling routes in Arroyo De La Encomienda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. Cyclists often praise the well-maintained rural roads, the blend of agricultural landscapes with historical towns, and the consistent, gradual gradients suitable for sustained efforts.

Q: Are there any hidden gem routes that are less frequented but offer great experiences?
A: While specific 'hidden gems' are subjective, exploring some of the less popular moderate routes, such as those venturing further into the agricultural plains or along smaller river tributaries, can offer a quieter and equally rewarding experience away from the main circuits.

Q: Is parking available near the starting points of road cycling routes?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in Arroyo De La Encomienda and the various towns and villages that serve as starting points for these routes. Look for public parking areas within these communities.

Q: Can I access road cycling routes using public transport?
A: Many of the routes connect to larger towns like Valladolid, which has good public transport links. While direct access to every trailhead via public transport might vary, you can often reach a nearby town and start your ride from there.
tips: Suspension bridge of modern architecture over the Pisuerga river in Valladolid. One of the 14 bridges that connect the south and north banks of the city.
Pedestrian bridge over the Pisuerga river. On the town side there is a bar and a restaurant with a good atmosphere.
Entering the town of Tordesillas, the medieval bridge over the Duero River offers incredible views of the city's landscape.

It is the place that throughout history has welcomed the community in its public activities: market, medieval games, bullfighting celebrations, etc.
Square of medieval origin. Its shape dates back to the time of the Catholic Monarchs, when they ordered the construction of a larger square in Tordesillas in accordance with the importance of the town. For this reason, a series of buildings will be demolished until the square acquires this almost square shape surrounded by arcades.

The building, constructed between the 16th and 18th centuries, began in the Gothic style and finished in the Neoclassical. Another striking feature is its tower, the tallest in the city.
The Baroque-era organ is considered one of the finest in the region, both for its design and its state of preservation.
Quiet country road towards Geria surrounded by cultivated fields. The landscape keeps changing with the seasons.
This impressive castle was built in the 15th century by the Admiral of Castilla Don Fadrique Enríquez, later it passed into the hands of the monarchy.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features asphalted cycling infrastructure, especially when departing from Valladolid. However, be prepared for some unpaved sections, particularly between Simancas and Tordesillas. While generally in good condition, some segments might be challenging to ride on a racebike.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route starts from Valladolid, specific parking for cyclists isn't detailed. For visiting the Castillo de Simancas, you can often find parking in the town of Simancas itself. For Valladolid, consider public parking garages or street parking near the starting point, such as the Hispanoamérica Bridge.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'moderate' for racebiking. It covers over 71 km with around 450m of elevation gain, requiring good physical fitness. Some unpaved sections can also add to the challenge, making it less ideal for beginners.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle in the Valladolid province are typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the public roads and paths in Castilla y León.

faqs.question: What historical sites and landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in history and sights. You'll pass by the impressive Castillo de Simancas, a 15th-century castle. In Tordesillas, you can explore the Main Square, the Church of Santa María la Mayor de la Asunción de Tordesillas, and the historic 'treaty houses' where the Treaty of Tordesillas was signed. You'll also cross the scenic Medieval Bridge of Tordesillas over the Duero River.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, portions of this route overlap with the natural path Senda del Duero (GR14) and connect with the Camino de Santiago de Madrid in Simancas. You may also encounter sections of the Cañada Real Burgalesa and the Ruta del Duero.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Simancas and Tordesillas offer various options for cafes, restaurants, and places to refuel. Tordesillas is also part of the Rueda wine route, providing opportunities to experience local gastronomy and wines.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route uses public roads and paths, it is primarily designed for racebiking. Bringing a dog, especially on a leash, would be challenging and potentially unsafe given the distance, speed, and potential for traffic. It's generally not recommended for this type of cycling route.

faqs.question: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots on the route?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. You'll get beautiful panoramic views of Tordesillas from the majestic Medieval Bridge of Tordesillas over the Duero River. The View of the Simancas Bridge also offers a picturesque scene, and the countryside of the Valladolid province provides pleasant scenery throughout.

faqs.question: What public transport options are available to reach the starting point in Valladolid or towns along the route?
faqs.answer: Valladolid is well-connected by regional and national bus and train services. You can take public transport to Valladolid city center to begin your ride. Buses also connect Valladolid with Simancas and Tordesillas, which could be useful for logistics or if you need to shorten your ride.

waypoints.name: Church of Santa María la Mayor de la Asunción de Tordesillas
